Details:
Despite a comprehensive literature search, removal data for E. coli O157:H7 at the full-scale level for conventional treatment was not found.
Removal of E. coli O157:H7 by simulated conventional treatment (coagulation, clarification and filtration) was found to be minimally effective. An analysis of 46 pilot-scale effluent samples generated under different treatment conditions demonstrated that E. coli O157:H7 had the lowest mean log removal, 0.7, compared to four other waterborne microbes (Aeromonas hydrophila, MS2, Cryptosporidium parvum, Encephalitozoan intestinalis) [854]. In the same study, breakthrough for E. coli O157:H7 was observed within one hour of filter run time [853, 854].
Poor filterability of E. coli O157:H7 may be explained by hydrodynamic theory, which predict that a particle size of 1 μm will be the most difficult to remove in granular media filtration. However, pathogens associated with floc particles will be better removed based on altered size and surface charge. Achieving a turbidity target of 0.2 NTU or less has been shown to improve pathogen removal [854].
